title: AI-Powered Philanthropy: Carryover Effects on Volunteer Engagement

study_design: We adopt a multi-method approach by first modeling how the introduction of AI affects a volunteer's utility and its implications for their future engagement.
Using a difference-in-differences (DID) design, we causally estimate the carryover effect of AI introduction on volunteer engagement after the adoption period.

We focus on carryover effects rather than direct effects during the adoption period period for two main reasons. First, the disaster response period involves higher volunteer activity due to the urgency of the disaster rather than typical volunteering motivations, making carryover effects a cleaner measure of AI's impact on future volunteer engagement.
Our quasi-natural experiment emerged during the Turkey-Syria earthquake in February 2023, when HOTTM faced an urgent need to process large amounts of mapping data quickly.
Because HOTTM set the default editor, volunteers contributing to these projects were externally introduced to Rapid, regardless of their prior editor preferences.
We define the period from February 6 to March 7, 2023, as the adoption period during which this systematic introduction of AI occurred.

To test the existence of the impact philanthropy effect among volunteers and to understand the effect of AI on them, we first create composite scores from participant responses in the third component of the experiment.
To address these challenges, we adopt a changes-in-changes (CIC) estimation approach using our full sample of 836 treated volunteers and 32,298 control volunteers.
task: HOTTM volunteers' primary task is to trace infrastructure (e.g., buildings, roads, waterways) on satellite imagery of the areas of interest using a mapping editor.

To contribute, volunteers log onto HOTTM and select a project that they want to work on. Upon clicking on a project's page, volunteers can then read a description of the project and what infrastructure needs to be mapped.
volunteers contribute to a project by selecting a task and tracing the required infrastructure (e.g., buildings, roads, waterways) on satellite imagery of the areas of interest within it.
The voluntary task asks participants to create a social media post for the NPO's outreach campaign. This task is unpaid and optional; participants may skip it and proceed to the third component.

Both conditions receive identical instructions: participants view sample social media posts demonstrating the desired content and writing style of the NPO. Next, participants are instructed to write a social media post to accompany an image about the NPO.
population: Between 2022 and 2023 alone, 645,000 volunteers contributed to 3,000 projects on the platform (HOT 2023).

We identify 2,803 volunteers who used Rapid for the first time during the adoption period. To causally measure how volunteer engagement changes after being introduced to Rapid, we retain only those 836 volunteers who made contributions to HOTTM before they ever used Rapid, that is, they contributed to the platform prior to the adoption period.

For our control group, we consider active volunteers who are not part of the treated group and contributed to HOTTM prior to the adoption period. We define an active volunteer as someone who has contributed to HOTTM within 431 days of February 6, 2023, a threshold that is three standard deviations longer than the average time in between volunteering sessions on HOTTM prior to February 6, 2023 (Ren et al. 2012, Urrea and Yoo 2023).
Our final dataset consists of 836 treated volunteers who performed 151,929 tasks and 32,298 control volunteers who performed 1,156,124 tasks during the observation window.

By applying CEM, we construct a control group that consists of volunteers who were not introduced to AI but demonstrate comparable engagement and experience on HOTTM to the treated volunteers who were introduced to AI, all measured prior to the adoption period. This matching process leads to a matched sample of 360 treated volunteers and 360 control volunteers.

We recruit 600 participants (49.5% female, Mage = 44.2, SDage = 13.5) for the online experiment. The average completion time is 10.9 minutes (i.e., an average hourly payment of $11.). Of these 600 participants, 108 participants (58.3% female, Mage = 46.0, SDage = 14.4) choose to do the volunteering task, and 55 participants are randomly assigned to the control condition and 53 to the AI-assisted condition.
arms: We define a treated volunteer as someone who used Rapid for the first time during the adoption period.
For our control group, we consider active volunteers who are not part of the treated group and contributed to HOTTM prior to the adoption period.

Control participants receive an empty text box to write the social media post from scratch. AI-assisted participants find the text box pre-filled with content generated using ChatGPT's GPT-4o free version, following the same instructions given to participants.
55 participants are randomly assigned to the control condition and 53 to the AI-assisted condition.
outcome_definitions: Our dependent variable, Tasksit, is operationalized as the number of tasks completed on HOTTM by volunteer i in month t. The number of tasks completed serves as a quantitative measure of volunteer participation and engagement on the platform (Burtch et al. 2013, Baruch et al. 2016, Urrea and Yoo 2023).

They indicate agreement with statements on a scale of 1-7 (1 = Strongly disagree; 7 = Strongly agree): "Volunteering my time makes a difference in the lives of the kids served by the Educational NPO," "Volunteering for the Educational NPO is an effective way for me to make a difference," "It is easy for me to imagine how volunteering my time will benefit the Educational NPO," "I put significant effort into helping the Educational NPO," and "I was thorough in helping the Educational NPO" (adapted from Duncan 2004, Buell et al. 2017, Mejia et al. 2019, Buell et al. 2021). These statements appear in random order to the participants. The first three statements capture perceived impact of volunteering while the last two assess the perceived effort.

We average the three perceived impact of volunteering statements into a single score called PIVi and the two perceived effort statements into PEi. Cronbach's alphas of 0.88 and 0.84, respectively, indicate high internal consistency.
statistical_results: Based on its negative and statistically significant estimate, we find that introduction to AI leads to a negative carryover effect (−0.240, p <0.01). Specifically, compared to a control volunteer, a treated volunteer reduces their monthly engagement by an average of 21.3%.
From the graph, we observe that the negative effect of AI persists across all months after the treatment.

The results support the parallel trends assumption, as none of the coefficients are statistically significant, confirming that treated and control volunteers follow similar engagement trends prior to AI introduction.

We find that AI-assisted volunteers report a lower perceived effort (4.6, S.E. = 0.237) compared to the control condition (5.6, S.E. = 0.150), and this difference is statistically significant ( p <0.01).

AI-assisted volunteers report a lower perceived impact of volunteering (5.0, S.E. = 0.206) compared to the control condition (5.5, S.E. = 0.151), and this difference is statistically significant ( p <0.05).

At the lower quantiles, representing less experienced volunteers, AI introduction has large negative effects on task contributions. Specifically, volunteers at the 20th percentile reduce their contributions by 19.99 tasks per week ( p <0.05), while those at the 30th, 40th, and 50th percentiles exhibit reductions of 21.37 ( p <0.01), 18.02 ( p <0.01), and 16.39 ( p <0.01) tasks after the adoption period, respectively.
The negative effect persists through the 60th percentile with a reduction of 7.34 tasks ( p <0.05).
At the 70th percentile, the effect becomes positive but statistically insignificant (2.32 tasks; p = 0.530).
volunteers at the 80th percentile increase contributions by 12.97 tasks (p <0.01), and those at the 90th percentile do so by 21.69 tasks ( p <0.01).
We estimate that after being introduced to AI, volunteers reduce their contributions by 21.3% on average.
In practical terms, this finding indicates that to maintain the volunteer workforce's average engagement prior to the introduction of AI, NPOs will need to recruit 27.1%5 more new volunteers.
ambiguity_or_caveats: Although volunteers could not distinguish this default setting until after selecting a project and reading the instructions, more active volunteers may have had a higher probability of encountering a Rapid-default project simply due to their greater participation.

That said, we acknowledge that the treatment may not be exogenous since volunteers did not receive treatment randomly. Instead, volunteers entered the treatment condition by choosing a project that had Rapid set as the default editor.

While this does not entirely eliminate the possibility of selection bias, it mitigates the concern because volunteers are effectively choosing projects without knowing prior knowledge of whether they would be prompted to use Rapid.

It is possible that once volunteers selected which projects to contribute to, they could choose not to use Rapid, even if it was set as the default editor. This is not a major concern, as OSM data reveal that the vast majority of treated volunteers (95.6%) in our sample used the default editor.

First, we only focus on volunteers being introduced to AI by utilizing Rapid to finish a task or a portion of a task at least once. Within a single task, volunteers could begin by using Rapid and then deactivate it for the remainder of the task. While the platform logs some information related to task duration, these measures are not sufficiently precise to capture how long volunteers actually spend using Rapid within each task. Our data do not provide insights into the proportion of time or effort within tasks that was performed with Rapid versus not.

Second, we do not account for the quality of the predicted suggestions by Rapid, and the quality of AI tools may impact AI adoption (Kwon et al. 2023). Unfortunately, the platform lacks objective quality measures, and currently, there is no available data to assess the quality of Rapid's predicted suggestions against such measures. Thus, it is possible that volunteers may react differently when introduced to AI tools performing much better or worse than the volunteers themselves. Given the current lack of data, AI quality's impact on volunteer engagement is beyond this project's scope, and we call on future research to explore this topic.
On HOTTM, experience is highly skewed — almost 99% of the volunteers have very little experience volunteering on the platform (Urrea and Yoo 2023).

When we apply our CEM procedure to create comparable treatment and control groups within experience categories, we lose substantial power due to the restrictive nature of matching across multiple variables on already small sub-samples.

notes: This is an empirical study combining a quasi-natural experiment (difference-in-differences design) using data from the Humanitarian OpenStreetMap Team Tasking Manager (HOTTM) platform, a controlled online experiment to test mechanisms, and a changes-in-changes (CIC) analysis for heterogeneous treatment effects. The paper examines how introduction of an AI tool (Rapid) affects volunteer engagement on an online volunteering platform. The main finding is a negative carryover effect of AI introduction on volunteer engagement (21.3% reduction on average), with heterogeneous effects by volunteer experience level: negative for inexperienced volunteers, positive for highly experienced volunteers. The mechanism is theorized to be impact philanthropy—volunteers' motivation to personally make a meaningful difference.
